label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what we can infer from the passage about volleyball. The options are A to D. First, I need to go through the passage again to make sure I understand the key points. The passage starts by saying volleyball was created in 1895 by William G. Morgan, who was influenced by basketball and James Naismith. He wanted a game suitable for middle-aged men, so it wasn't as fast as basketball. He created "mintonette," which later became volleyball. The original game used hands instead of equipment to cut costs, and it was similar to badminton but with a net in the middle. The game became popular, spread widely, and had several rule changes. They set specific rules for court size, number of players, hits per team, rotation, and standardized the ball. The rules kept changing until the modern game. Now, it's popular across all age groups and has various competitive teams. Now looking at the options: A) Volleyball develops from the game of "mintonette". The passage says Morgan created "mintonette," which became volleyball. So A seems correct because mintonette is the predecessor. The passage says "he created the game of 'mintonette.' Volleyball history shows..." implying that volleyball developed from mintonette. B) Most competitive teams are in college. The passage mentions high school, college, women, men, children, mixed teams, etc., but it doesn't say most are in college. It just lists them as existing, not the majority. So B is likely incorrect. C) Rules changed because they are easy to learn. The passage says rules continued to change until the game became what we have today. The reason for changes isn't directly stated as being easy to learn. The part about being easy to learn is in the last paragraph, stating the game's popularity due to being easy to learn, but that's not the reason for rule changes. So C seems incorrect. D) Different rules to cut costs. The original cost-cutting was using hands instead of equipment, but the rules mentioned later (court size, rotation, etc.)
